feedback.text: Hello. I believe these are very important topics, and the EU might be the only major power realistically able to make real progress in securing our consumer rights in these regards. In particular, the point of "addictive design of digital products", stands out to me. I enjoy video games, as many people do, but in the recent decade they have undoubtedly made a turn for the worse. Large companies are designing their games ON PURPOSE to milk as much money from their consumers as possible, by hiding game content behind paid chance mechanics like loot boxes, or more recently, gacha. These mechanics categorically decrease the quality and enjoyment of the games they're in, no matter how much these corporations try to argue in court that "actually players enjoy this more". No we don't. It's a skinnerbox. They're putting people, often even children, in a digital skinnerbox because they know these chance mechanics are addictive and get people to blow more money on things that realistically, they already paid for buying the videogame. Across the internet, rights like privacy, anonymity, and freedom are on the decline. We see it in the UK right now, with their incredibly invasive age verification rules. Anti-consumer practices like these are on the rise, not just in videogames, but that is a prominent example. It is incredibly important to me as an EU citizen, that the EU does something about this. The EU is the one governing body that I trust in, to be able to safeguard our consumer rights, while also protecting our human freedoms. Please consider that. Thank you.
